title: The Neuroscience of Decision-Making
description: For questions 17 - 24, read the text below. Use the word given in capitals at the end of some of the lines to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Scientists have long been fascinated by the (0) REMARKABLE complexity of human decision-making. Recent advances in brain imaging have revealed a clear (17) between emotional and rational systems in the brain, suggesting that choices rarely emerge from logic alone. This has led to the (18) of a new model in which emotion and reason are treated as equally important inputs to any decision. || Under conditions of stress, decision-making can become severely (19), as elevated cortisol levels interfere with the functioning of the prefrontal cortex. Neuroscientists argue that an individual's (20) to manage such pressures varies considerably from person to person. They have made (21) progress in identifying the specific neural circuits involved in these processes, though a complete understanding remains some way off. || These insights have clear (22) for a range of applied fields. Practitioners in medicine, law and economics are now revisiting assumptions that were once treated as (23). It appears almost (24) that future research will continue to transform our understanding of how choices are made.

example.text: Scientists have long been fascinated by the (0) REMARKABLE complexity of human decision-making.

questions.explanation: Requires a noun formed by replacing the verb ending with '-ion', meaning a separation or distinction between two things.
Requires a noun formed by adding the suffix '-ence' to the verb stem, meaning the process of coming into existence.
Requires a participial adjective (past participle used predicatively after 'become'), meaning weakened or damaged.
Requires a noun formed by replacing '-le' with '-lity' (with internal spelling adjustment), meaning the capacity or power to do something.
Requires an adjective formed by adding the suffix '-able', meaning notably large or significant in degree.
Requires a plural noun formed from the verb via the '-ication' change and a plural suffix, meaning likely consequences or effects.
Requires an adjective formed with the negative prefix 'in-' and the suffix '-able' (removing the final '-e'), meaning not open to question or challenge.
Requires an adjective formed with the negative prefix 'un-' and the suffix '-able', meaning impossible to prevent or escape.
